---
title: "Measurement Error in Causal Inference"
author: "Bielicke, L. and Xochihua-Tlecuitl, T."
date: today
jupyter: python3
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-title: "Contents"
    theme: cosmo
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: false
    self-contained: true
    html-math-method: mathjax
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

```{python}
import numpy as np
import plotly.graph_objects as go
from IPython.display import display, HTML

def show(fig):
    display(HTML(fig.to_html(full_html=False, include_plotlyjs="cdn")))

rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
```

::: {.callout-note icon=false}
## Key Ideas

By the end of this tutorial you should be able to:

1. Define measurement error 
2. Describe how classical measurement error influences treatment, conditioning,
and outcome variables differently
4. Apply instrumental variables and 2SLS to recover unbiased estimates
5. Operationalize constructs as latent variables
6. Ensure measurement models meet specific invariance requirements 
:::

---

## 1. Measurement Error: Definitions & Types
**Measurement
error** is the misalignment between the true state of a variable and its reported
state. We often assume that the variables we reason about are the variables
we observe. This assumption rarely holds in practice. Instead, we observe some
*proxy variable*
i.e., a noisy or imperfect stand-in for the true variable of interest. 

Understanding measurement error is important because the variable we draw
conclusions *with* is not always the variable we draw conclusions *about*.
To identify unbiased causal estimates, we need to understand the relationship
between the variable of interest and a noisy proxy. 


### 1.1 Motivating Example: Subjective Well-Being

Researchers are studying the effect of happiness on physical activity. Because
they cannot measure partipants' happiness directly, they use a self-reported to
determine happiness. The researchers believe that the self-reports, though not a
perfect representation of happiness, correlate with partipants true happiness
levels. 

```{=html}
<style>
.swls-item { border: 0.5px solid var(--color-border-tertiary); border-radius: 12px; padding: 1rem 1.25rem; margin-bottom: 12px; background: #fff; }
.swls-q { font-size: 15px; font-weight: 500; margin: 0 0 12px; }
.swls-labels { display: flex; justify-content: space-between; font-size: 11px; color: #888; margin-top: 6px; }
.swls-btns { display: flex; gap: 6px; flex-wrap: wrap; }
.swls-btn { flex: 1; min-width: 36px; padding: 6px 4px; font-size: 13px; border: 0.5px solid #ccc; border-radius: 8px; background: transparent; color: #555; cursor: pointer; transition: background 0.1s; }
.swls-btn:hover { background: #f5f5f5; }
.swls-btn.selected { background: #EEEDFE; border-color: #534AB7; color: #3C3489; font-weight: 500; }
.score-card { border-radius: 12px; padding: 1.25rem 1.5rem; margin-top: 1.5rem; background: #f7f7f7; border: 0.5px solid #ddd; }
.score-num { font-size: 36px; font-weight: 500; }
.score-label { font-size: 14px; color: #666; margin-top: 4px; }
.score-bar-bg { height: 8px; border-radius: 4px; background: #e0e0e0; margin: 12px 0 4px; }
.score-bar-fill { height: 8px; border-radius: 4px; background: #534AB7; transition: width 0.3s; }
.score-range { display: flex; justify-content: space-between; font-size: 11px; color: #aaa; }
.swls-note { font-size: 12px; color: #aaa; margin-top: 1rem; line-height: 1.5; }
</style>

<div style="padding: 1rem 0;">

<div class="swls-item">
  <p class="swls-q">1. In most ways my life is close to my ideal.</p>
  <div class="swls-btns" id="q1"></div>
  <div class="swls-labels"><span>Strongly disagree</span><span>Strongly agree</span></div>
</div>

<div class="swls-item">
  <p class="swls-q">2. The conditions of my life are excellent.</p>
  <div class="swls-btns" id="q2"></div>
  <div class="swls-labels"><span>Strongly disagree</span><span>Strongly agree</span></div>
</div>

<div class="swls-item">
  <p class="swls-q">3. I am satisfied with my life.</p>
  <div class="swls-btns" id="q3"></div>
  <div class="swls-labels"><span>Strongly disagree</span><span>Strongly agree</span></div>
</div>

<div class="swls-item">
  <p class="swls-q">4. So far I have gotten the important things I want in life.</p>
  <div class="swls-btns" id="q4"></div>
  <div class="swls-labels"><span>Strongly disagree</span><span>Strongly agree</span></div>
</div>

<div class="swls-item">
  <p class="swls-q">5. If I could live my life over, I would change almost nothing.</p>
  <div class="swls-btns" id="q5"></div>
  <div class="swls-labels"><span>Strongly disagree</span><span>Strongly agree</span></div>
</div>

<div class="score-card">
  <div style="display:flex; align-items:baseline; gap:10px;">
    <span class="score-num" id="score-num">—</span>
    <span class="score-label" id="score-label">Answer all five items to see your score</span>
  </div>
  <div class="score-bar-bg"><div class="score-bar-fill" id="score-bar" style="width:0%"></div></div>
  <div class="score-range"><span>5</span><span>35</span></div>
</div>

<p class="swls-note">Diener, E., Emmons, R. A., Larsen, R. J., &amp; Griffin, S. (1985). The Satisfaction with Life Scale. <em>Journal of Personality Assessment, 49</em>(1), 71–75. Free to use for research and educational purposes with attribution.</p>

</div>

<script>
const bands = [
  [31,35,'Extremely satisfied'],
  [26,30,'Satisfied'],
  [21,25,'Slightly satisfied'],
  [20,20,'Neutral'],
  [15,19,'Slightly dissatisfied'],
  [10,14,'Dissatisfied'],
  [5,9,'Extremely dissatisfied']
];
const answers = {q1:null,q2:null,q3:null,q4:null,q5:null};

['q1','q2','q3','q4','q5'].forEach(id => {
  const container = document.getElementById(id);
  ['1','2','3','4','5','6','7'].forEach((l,i) => {
    const btn = document.createElement('button');
    btn.className = 'swls-btn';
    btn.textContent = l;
    btn.onclick = () => {
      container.querySelectorAll('.swls-btn').forEach(b => b.classList.remove('selected'));
      btn.classList.add('selected');
      answers[id] = i + 1;
      update();
    };
    container.appendChild(btn);
  });
});

function update() {
  const vals = Object.values(answers);
  if (vals.some(v => v === null)) return;
  const total = vals.reduce((a,b) => a+b, 0);
  document.getElementById('score-num').textContent = total;
  const band = bands.find(([lo,hi]) => total >= lo && total <= hi);
  document.getElementById('score-label').textContent = band ? band[2] : '';
  document.getElementById('score-bar').style.width = ((total - 5) / 30 * 100).toFixed(1) + '%';
}
</script>
```


::: {.callout-note}
## Note
Prior work shows that single scale surveys correlate with true metrics
at roughly $r = 0.5$, while multi-item scales reach about $r = 0.8$. Neither
is perfect!
:::


### 1.2 Classical Measurement Error
Classical measurement error occurs when the observed variable equals the the true value **plus**
some noise:

$$D^* = D + U$$

where the error term $U$ satisfies four key assumptions:

- $\mathbb{E}(U) = 0$ (zero mean)
- $U \perp D$ (independent of the true value)
- $U \perp Y$ (independent of the outcome)
- $U \perp Z$ (independent of any covariate)

The DAG below encodes the independence assumptions. 

```{dot}
//| label: fig-dag-basic
//| fig-cap: "We observe Y* (the proxy) but we are interested in Y."
//| fig-height: 1.6
digraph {
  rankdir=LR
  node [fontname="Helvetica" fontsize=13]
  D      [shape=square]
  Y      [shape=circle style=dashed]
  Ystar  [shape=square  label="Y*"]
  U [shape=circle  style=dashed label="U" width=0.45 height=0.45 fixedsize=true fontsize=10]
  D -> Y
  Y -> Ystar
  U -> Ystar [style=dashed]
}
```

There are other types of measurmemnt error (e.g., differential). We will focus
on classicial measurement error throughout this tutorial. 

::: {.callout-note}
## Question

Can a binary variable have classical ME? Why or why not?
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Answer
No. Classical ME requires $U \perp Z$, but if $Z$ is binary ($Z \in \{0, 1\}$),
the error term is necessarily correlated with $Z$. For example, since $U = Z* - Z$, if Z
is 1 then Z* must be 0 and U is -1, and if Z is 0 then Z* must be 1 and U is 1. 
:::

#### Other Examples of Classical ME
- Blood pressure recorded at a single clinic visit
- Self-reported hours worked
- Human error when reading time from a clock
- Mechanical error from scales

---

## 2. Causal Estimand, Identification & Attenuation Bias

### 2.1 Setup

Throughout the tutorial, we are interested in measuring the average treatment
effect (ATE) of $D$ on $Y$. Most of our results assume a linear regression
model. Below, we explain how measurement error in treatment, conditioning, and
outcome variables can influence findings.

### 2.2 Classical ME in Treatment: Attenuation Bias
We want to determine $\beta$ in $Y = \beta D + \varepsilon$, but we
observe only $D^* = D + U$. Plugging the proxy
into OLS gives:

$$\hat{\beta} = \frac{\text{Cov}(Y,\, D^*)}{\text{Var}(D^*)} =
\frac{\text{Cov}(\beta D + \varepsilon,\; D + U)}{\text{Var}(D) + \text{Var}(U)}
= \frac{\beta\,\text{Var}(D)}{\text{Var}(D) + \text{Var}(U)} = \beta\lambda$$

where $\lambda = \text{Var}(D) / (\text{Var}(D) + \text{Var}(U))$ is the
**reliability ratio**. In the derivation above, we leverage the classical
ME assumptions that $\text{Cov}(D, U) = 0$ and
$\text{Cov}(\varepsilon, U) = 0$.


::: {.callout-note}
## Question

In which direction is $\hat{\beta}$ biased when there is classical measurement
error in $D$? (Hint: what is the value of $\lambda$?)
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Answer
Because $\lambda < 1$, the OLS estimate attenuates toward zero. For example, when $\lambda = 0.5$, a true effect of $\beta = 0.6$ appears as $\hat{\beta} \approx 0.3$.
:::


::: {.callout-tip}
## Attenuation Bias

Attenuation bias occurs when a regressor's estimated coefficient is biased
toward zero. Measurement error on treatment variables results in attenuation
bias.
:::

Explore the chart below to see how the reliability ratio affects our causal
estimate. Notice that when the reliability ratio gets smaller, the fitted slope
shrinks toward zero even as the true relationship stays fixed. In other words,
the causal estimate is attenuated.

```{python}
#| label: fig-attenuation
#| fig-cap: "Attenuation bias as reliability decreases. The sage line is the true slope; the purple dashed line is what OLS recovers from the noisy proxy."

n = 50
beta_true = 1.0
lambdas = np.round(np.arange(0.10, 1.01, 0.05), 2)
dline = np.linspace(-3.5, 3.5, 120)

# Pre-compute all per-λ values so we can build background lines
lam_data = []
for lam in lambdas:
    var_u  = (1 - lam) / lam
    D_true = rng.normal(0, 1, n)
    U      = rng.normal(0, np.sqrt(var_u), n)
    D_obs  = D_true + U
    Y      = beta_true * D_true + rng.normal(0, 1, n)
    b_hat  = np.cov(Y, D_obs)[0, 1] / np.var(D_obs)
    b_th   = beta_true * lam
    lam_data.append((D_obs, Y, b_hat, b_th))

# K independent OLS simulations per λ — background updates with the slider
K      = 5
bg_rng = np.random.default_rng(99)
bg_hats = {}
for lam in lambdas:
    var_u = (1 - lam) / lam
    hats  = []
    for _ in range(K):
        Dt = bg_rng.normal(0, 1, n)
        U  = bg_rng.normal(0, np.sqrt(max(var_u, 0)), n)
        Do = Dt + U
        Yb = beta_true * Dt + bg_rng.normal(0, 1, n)
        hats.append(np.cov(Yb, Do)[0, 1] / np.var(Do))
    bg_hats[float(lam)] = hats

# Background traces first (indices 0–K-1); foreground at K–K+3
D_obs_i, Y_i, b_hat_i, b_th_i = lam_data[-1]
init_bg = [
    go.Scatter(x=dline, y=bg_hats[float(lambdas[-1])][k] * dline, mode="lines",
               line=dict(color="rgba(138,110,181,0.25)", width=1.5),
               showlegend=False, hoverinfo="skip")
    for k in range(K)
]
init_fg = [
    go.Scatter(x=D_obs_i, y=Y_i, mode="markers",
               marker=dict(size=3, color="#7a9baa", opacity=0.25),
               showlegend=False),
    go.Scatter(x=dline, y=beta_true * dline, mode="lines",
               name=f"True β = {beta_true}",
               line=dict(color="#739e7e", width=2.5)),
    go.Scatter(x=dline, y=b_hat_i * dline, mode="lines",
               name=f"OLS β̂ = {b_hat_i:.3f}",
               line=dict(color="#8a6eb5", width=2.5, dash="dash")),
    go.Scatter(x=dline, y=b_th_i * dline, mode="lines",
               name=f"Theory βλ = {b_th_i:.3f}",
               line=dict(color="#c4a8d4", width=1.5, dash="dot")),
]

frames, steps = [], []
for lam, (D_obs, Y, b_hat, b_th) in zip(lambdas, lam_data):
    bg_updates = [go.Scatter(y=bg_hats[float(lam)][k] * dline) for k in range(K)]
    frames.append(go.Frame(
        name=str(lam),
        data=bg_updates + [
            go.Scatter(x=D_obs, y=Y, mode="markers",
                       marker=dict(size=3, color="#7a9baa", opacity=0.25),
                       showlegend=False),
            go.Scatter(x=dline, y=beta_true * dline, mode="lines",
                       name=f"True β = {beta_true}",
                       line=dict(color="#739e7e", width=2.5)),
            go.Scatter(x=dline, y=b_hat * dline, mode="lines",
                       name=f"OLS β̂ = {b_hat:.3f}",
                       line=dict(color="#8a6eb5", width=2.5, dash="dash")),
            go.Scatter(x=dline, y=b_th * dline, mode="lines",
                       name=f"Theory βλ = {b_th:.3f}",
                       line=dict(color="#c4a8d4", width=1.5, dash="dot")),
        ]
    ))
    steps.append(dict(
        method="animate",
        label=str(lam),
        args=[[str(lam)], dict(mode="immediate",
                               frame=dict(duration=0, redraw=True),
                               transition=dict(duration=0))]
    ))

fig_att = go.Figure(data=init_bg + init_fg, frames=frames)
fig_att.update_layout(
    xaxis=dict(title="Observed D*", range=[-4, 4]),
    yaxis=dict(title="Y", range=[-4, 4]),
    legend=dict(x=0.02, y=0.98, bgcolor="rgba(255,255,255,0.7)"),
    margin=dict(t=40, b=80),
    sliders=[dict(
        active=len(lambdas) - 1,
        currentvalue=dict(prefix="Reliability λ = ", visible=True, xanchor="center"),
        pad=dict(b=10, t=40),
        steps=steps
    )],
    height=440,
)
show(fig_att)
```

### 2.3 ME in Outcome Variables
We *still* want to determine $\beta$ in $Y = \beta D + \varepsilon$. This time, we
observe a noisy outcome $Y^* = Y + U$. Plugging the proxy
into OLS gives:

$$Y^* = \beta D + (\varepsilon + U)$$

The measurement noise is absorbed entirely into the composite residual
$\varepsilon + U$. Because $U$ is independent of $D$, the relationship between
$D$ and $Y$ is unbiased. However, the residual variance increases from
$\sigma^2_\varepsilon$ to $\sigma^2_\varepsilon + \sigma^2_U$, which inflates
standard errors and reduces $R^2$.

### Exercise: Outcome measurement error
The following exercise shows how analysis with a noisy measure of $Y$ compares
to analysis with the ground truth measure.

The `gpa1` dataset (Wooldridge) records self-reported college GPA ($Y$) and
weekly study hours ($D$) for 141 undergraduates. Self-reported GPA is subject
to small rounding and recall errors. We treat the recorded GPA as ground truth
and simulate a noisy proxy:

$$\text{GPA}^*_i = \text{GPA}_i + U_i, \quad U_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\,0.3^2)$$

<details>
<summary>Load the data in R</summary>

```r
library(wooldridge)
d <- na.omit(gpa1[, c("colGPA", "study")])
set.seed(5)
d$gpa_noisy <- d$colGPA + rnorm(nrow(d), mean = 0, sd = 0.3)
```

</details>

**Question 1.** Compute the measurement error $U = Y^* - Y$ (`gpa_noisy` minus
`colGPA`). What is the mean and standard deviation?

```r
err <- d$gpa_noisy - d$colGPA
mean(err)
sd(err)
```

**Question 2.** Check the correlation between `colGPA` and `gpa_noisy`.

```r
cor(d$colGPA, d$gpa_noisy)
```

**Question 3.** Regress GPA on study hours using `colGPA` and then using
`gpa_noisy`. Compare the slope coefficients, standard errors, and $R^2$.

```r
summary(lm(colGPA    ~ study, data = d))
summary(lm(gpa_noisy ~ study, data = d))
```

::: {.callout-tip icon=false collapse=true}
## Solution

```r
library(wooldridge)
d <- na.omit(gpa1[, c("colGPA", "study")])
set.seed(5)
d$gpa_noisy <- d$colGPA + rnorm(nrow(d), mean = 0, sd = 0.3)

# error stats
err <- d$gpa_noisy - d$colGPA
mean(err)   # ≈ 0.0
sd(err)     # ≈ 0.1

# correlation
cor(d$colGPA, d$gpa_noisy)   # ≈ 0.80

# regression
summary(lm(colGPA    ~ study, data = d))
# slope ≈ 0.046,  SE ≈ 0.029,  R² ≈ 0.017

summary(lm(gpa_noisy ~ study, data = d))
# slope ≈ 0.046,  SE ≈ 0.036,  R² ≈ 0.011
```
:::

The two slopes are identical because ME in the outcome does not
introduce bias. The standard error increases and $R^2$ falls. A noisier outcome means you
need a larger sample to achieve the same statistical precision.

### Simulation

Each point below is one observation plotted as (true $Y$, observed $Y^*$). Drag
the slider to add noise to the outcome variable. Notice that the mean stays near zero while
the standard deviation grows.

```{python}
#| label: fig-outcome-me
#| fig-cap: "Classical ME in the outcome: true Y (x-axis) vs observed Y* (y-axis). The error mean stays ~0 while the SD grows with noise."

n = 100
beta_true = 0.6
sigma_y_vals = np.round(np.arange(0.0, 2.05, 0.1), 1)
D_ome = rng.normal(0, 1, n)
eps_ome = rng.normal(0, 1, n)
Y_true_ome = beta_true * D_ome + eps_ome

frames_ome, steps_ome = [], []
for sigma in sigma_y_vals:
    U_Y = rng.normal(0, sigma, n) if sigma > 0 else np.zeros(n)
    Y_obs_ome = Y_true_ome + U_Y
    err_mean = float(np.mean(U_Y))
    err_sd   = float(np.std(U_Y))
    corr_yy  = float(np.corrcoef(Y_true_ome, Y_obs_ome)[0, 1])

    frames_ome.append(go.Frame(
        name=str(sigma),
        data=[
            go.Scatter(
                x=Y_true_ome, y=Y_obs_ome, mode="markers",
                marker=dict(size=4, color="#7a9baa", opacity=0.35),
                name="Observations", showlegend=True,
            ),
            go.Scatter(
                x=[-4, 4], y=[-4, 4], mode="lines",
                name="Perfect measurement",
                line=dict(color="gray", width=1.5, dash="dash"),
                showlegend=True,
            ),
            go.Scatter(
                x=[None], y=[None], mode="markers",
                marker=dict(opacity=0),
                name=f"Error mean = {err_mean:+.3f}   SD = {err_sd:.3f}   r(Y, Y*) = {corr_yy:.3f}",
                showlegend=True,
            ),
        ]
    ))
    steps_ome.append(dict(
        method="animate", label=str(sigma),
        args=[[str(sigma)], dict(mode="immediate",
                                  frame=dict(duration=0, redraw=True),
                                  transition=dict(duration=0))]
    ))

fig_ome = go.Figure(data=frames_ome[0].data, frames=frames_ome)
fig_ome.update_layout(
    xaxis=dict(title="True outcome  Y", range=[-5, 5]),
    yaxis=dict(title="Observed outcome  Y*", range=[-7, 7]),
    legend=dict(x=0.02, y=0.98, bgcolor="rgba(255,255,255,0.75)"),
    margin=dict(t=40, b=80),
    sliders=[dict(
        active=0,
        currentvalue=dict(prefix="Noise σ = ", visible=True, xanchor="center"),
        pad=dict(b=10, t=40),
        steps=steps_ome,
    )],
    height=440,
)
show(fig_ome)
```

---

## 2.4 Measurement Error in Conditioning Variables
We *still* want to determine the ATE of $D$ on $Y$. This time, we assume there
is some confounding variable $Z$. Unfortunately, We only observe a noisy $Z^* =
Z + U$ where $U_Z$ is classical ME (mean zero, independent of $D$, $Y$, and $Z$).

We want to determine $\beta_Z$ in:

$$Y = \beta_D D + \beta_Z Z + \varepsilon_1 $$

But we only observe $\beta_{Z^*}$ in:

$$Y = \beta_D D + \beta_{Z^*} Z^* + \varepsilon_2 $$

$\beta_{Z^*}$ is biased towards zero as in the bivariate regression, but what happens to $\beta_D$? We can see directly from
the DAG structure that $\beta_D$ is biased: there is an open backdoor back from
$D$ to $Y$ through $Z$, even after conditioning on $Z^*$:


```{dot}
//| label: fig-dag-conditioning
//| fig-cap: "Classical ME in the conditioning variable: controlling for Z* instead of Z leaves the backdoor path X ← Z → Y open."
//| fig-height: 2.2
digraph {
  rankdir=LR
  node [fontname="Helvetica" fontsize=13]
  Z   [shape=circle style=dashed label="Z", width=0.9 height=0.9]
  Zs  [shape=square  label="Z*"]
  U [shape=circle  style=dashed,  width=0.45 height=0.45 fixedsize=true fontsize=10]
  X   [shape=square  label="X"]
  Y   [shape=square  label="Y"]
  Z  -> X
  Z  -> Y
  Z  -> Zs  [style=dashed label="P(z*|z)"]
  U -> Zs [style=dashed]
  X  -> Y
}
```


### 2.4.1 Bias in Multivariate OLS
What can we know about the direction of the bias and the relationship to
$\beta_Z$? Let's explore how classical ME in a *single variable* affects
coefficients in a multivariate OLS. First, we will show that $\beta_{Z^*}$ is
biased towards zero. Then, we will show that $\beta_{D}$ is biased in an
unpredictable direction. Specifically, the bias in $\beta_{D}$ depends on the
direction of $\beta_Z$. 

Remember that the variation in $Z$ left after removing the component of $Z$ explained by
$D$ is the residual variance from regressing $Z$ on $D$:

$$\sigma^2_{Z|D} = \text{Var}(Z) - \text{Var}(\hat{Z}_{|D}) \leq \sigma^2_Z$$

The attenuation factor in the multivariate model becomes

$$\lambda_1 = \frac{\sigma^2_{Z|D}}{\sigma^2_{Z|D} + \sigma^2_U}$$

and 
$$\hat\beta_{Z^*} = \lambda_1\,\beta_Z$$

Because $\sigma^2_{Z|D} \leq \sigma^2_Z$ and $Z$ is correlated with $D$, we have
$\lambda_1 \leq \lambda$. This relationship reveals that classicial measurement
error in multivariate regression accentuates attenuation in the variable with
ME. 


Now, we will explore how this bias in $\beta_Z$ affects $\beta_D$. To do so, we need to decompose the bias in $\hat\beta_{D} = \beta_D + \text{bias}$. Intuitively, we need to know the relationship between $D$ and $Z$ and the relationship between $Z$ and $Z^*$. The combination of these relationships tells us how much unaccounted confounding influences $\hat\beta_D$.

Let $\delta$ be the slope from the regression of $Z$ (some confounding variable) on $D$ (i.e., $Z = \delta_0 + \delta D + \epsilon$). Then:

$$\hat\beta_{D} = \beta_D + (\beta_Z - \beta_{Z^*}) \delta = \beta_D + (1 - \lambda_1)\,\beta_Z\,\delta$$

::: {.callout-note}
## Exercise

1. What are the two components in the bias term $(1 - \lambda_1)\beta_Z\delta$?

2. What does $\delta$ represent? When is this term large?

3. What does $(1 - \lambda_1)\beta_Z$ represent? How does the sign of $\beta_Z$
affect the direction of bias in $\beta_D$?
:::

$\delta$ tells us the strength of the relationship between $Z$ and $D$.
$(1 - \lambda_1)\beta_Z$ is the bias introduced by measurement error. The bias
term $(1 - \lambda_1)\beta_Z\delta$ is non-zero whenever $D$ and $Z$ are
correlated ($\delta \neq 0$) and $Z$ has a true effect ($\beta_Z \neq 0$). In
other words, measurement error in a conditioning variable biases the treatment
coefficient, and the direction of bias depends on $\beta_Z$ and $\delta$.

Explore the graph below to see how measurement error in a conditioning variable
influence $\beta_D$. The starting DGP is $Y = \beta_D D + \beta_Z Z +
\varepsilon$ with $\beta_D = 1$ and $\beta_Z = 0.8$. 

Try adjusting $\beta_Z$ using the dropdown menu. What happens to
$\hat{\beta}_X$ when you flip the sign of $\beta_Z$? In which direction is
$\hat{\beta}_X$ biased for different values of $\beta_Z$?

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Answer

The bias on $\hat{\beta}_X$ is $(1 - \lambda_1)\beta_Z\delta$, so the
direction of bias depends on the sign of $\beta_Z$ and $\delta$. 

:::

```{python}
#| label: fig-conditioning-me
#| fig-cap: "ME in the conditioning variable. Use the dropdown to change β_Z in the DGP; drag the slider to change the reliability of Z*. Sage = true β_D = 1, purple = OLS estimate, lavender = theoretical prediction."

n       = 50
bD_true = 1.0
rho     = 0.85

rng2 = np.random.default_rng(7)
DZ   = rng2.multivariate_normal([0, 0], [[1, rho], [rho, 1]], n)
D_c  = DZ[:, 0]
Z_c  = DZ[:, 1]
eps  = rng2.normal(0, 1, n)   # fixed noise — same draw for every bZ

# Fixed ME noise for each lambda level so changing bZ doesn't reshuffle noise
rng3 = np.random.default_rng(42)
bz_vals   = [-0.8, -0.4, 0.0, 0.4, 0.8, 1.2, 1.6]
lambdas_c = np.round(np.arange(0.1, 1.01, 0.1), 2)
sigma2_ZD = 1 - rho**2
dline_c   = np.linspace(-3.5, 3.5, 120)

lam_noise = {}
for lam in lambdas_c:
    var_u = (1 - lam) / lam if lam < 1.0 else 0.0
    lam_noise[float(lam)] = rng3.normal(0, np.sqrt(max(var_u, 1e-9)), n)

def make_traces(bz, lam, D, Z, eps_arr):
    Y_bz   = bD_true * D + bz * Z + eps_arr
    var_u  = (1 - lam) / lam if lam < 1.0 else 0.0
    U_c    = lam_noise[float(lam)] if lam < 1.0 else np.zeros(n)
    Zs     = Z + U_c
    A      = np.column_stack([np.ones(n), D, Zs])
    coefs  = np.linalg.lstsq(A, Y_bz, rcond=None)[0]
    bD_hat = float(coefs[1])
    lam1   = sigma2_ZD / (sigma2_ZD + var_u) if var_u > 0 else 1.0
    bD_th  = bD_true + (1 - lam1) * bz * rho
    return Y_bz, bD_hat, bD_th

def build_avp(bz, lam):
    """Compute added-variable plot residuals and regression coefficients."""
    Y_bz, bD_hat, bD_th = make_traces(bz, lam, D_c, Z_c, eps)
    U_c = lam_noise[float(lam)] if lam < 1.0 else np.zeros(n)
    Zs  = Z_c + U_c
    A_z = np.column_stack([np.ones(n), Zs])
    M_D = D_c  - A_z @ np.linalg.lstsq(A_z, D_c,  rcond=None)[0]
    M_Y = Y_bz - A_z @ np.linalg.lstsq(A_z, Y_bz, rcond=None)[0]
    return M_D, M_Y, bD_hat, bD_th

mrange       = np.linspace(-3, 3, 120)
n_bz         = len(bz_vals)
lam_full_idx = len(lambdas_c) - 1
default_bzi  = bz_vals.index(0.8)

# Pre-compute all (bzi, lami) combinations
avp = {(bzi, lami): build_avp(bz, lam)
       for bzi, bz   in enumerate(bz_vals)
       for lami, lam in enumerate(lambdas_c)}

n_lam = len(lambdas_c)
K_c   = 5  # background draws per (bzi, lami)

# K_c independent fresh-dataset OLS estimates per (bzi, lami) — mirrors treatment sim.
bg_rng_c = np.random.default_rng(77)
bg_c_hats = {}
for bzi, bz in enumerate(bz_vals):
    for lami, lam in enumerate(lambdas_c):
        var_u = (1 - lam) / lam if lam < 1.0 else 0.0
        hats = []
        for _ in range(K_c):
            DZ_bg  = bg_rng_c.multivariate_normal([0, 0], [[1, rho], [rho, 1]], n)
            D_bg   = DZ_bg[:, 0]
            Z_bg   = DZ_bg[:, 1]
            U_bg   = bg_rng_c.normal(0, np.sqrt(max(var_u, 1e-9)), n)
            Y_bg   = bD_true * D_bg + bz * Z_bg + bg_rng_c.normal(0, 1, n)
            A_bg   = np.column_stack([np.ones(n), D_bg, Z_bg + U_bg])
            hats.append(float(np.linalg.lstsq(A_bg, Y_bg, rcond=None)[0][1]))
        bg_c_hats[(bzi, lami)] = hats

# Background: K_c traces per β_Z group → 35 total, indices 0–34.
bg_traces = []
for bzi in range(n_bz):
    vis = (bzi == default_bzi)
    for k in range(K_c):
        bD_bg = bg_c_hats[(bzi, lam_full_idx)][k]
        bg_traces.append(go.Scatter(
            x=list(mrange), y=list(bD_bg * mrange), mode="lines",
            line=dict(color="rgba(138,110,181,0.25)", width=1.5),
            visible=vis, showlegend=False, hoverinfo="skip",
        ))

# Foreground: 28 traces (4 per β_Z), indices 35–62.
fg_traces = []
for bzi in range(n_bz):
    M_D, M_Y, bD_hat, bD_th = avp[(bzi, lam_full_idx)]
    vis = (bzi == default_bzi)
    fg_traces += [
        go.Scatter(x=list(M_D), y=list(M_Y), mode="markers",
                   marker=dict(size=3, color="#7a9baa", opacity=0.25),
                   visible=vis, showlegend=False),
        go.Scatter(x=list(mrange), y=list(bD_true * mrange), mode="lines",
                   name=f"True β_D = {bD_true}",
                   line=dict(color="#739e7e", width=2.5),
                   visible=vis, showlegend=vis),
        go.Scatter(x=list(mrange), y=list(bD_hat * mrange), mode="lines",
                   name=f"OLS β̂_D = {bD_hat:.3f}",
                   line=dict(color="#8a6eb5", width=2.5, dash="dash"),
                   visible=vis, showlegend=vis),
        go.Scatter(x=list(mrange), y=list(bD_th * mrange), mode="lines",
                   name=f"Theory = {bD_th:.3f}",
                   line=dict(color="#c4a8d4", width=1.5, dash="dot"),
                   visible=vis, showlegend=vis),
    ]

init_traces = bg_traces + fg_traces  # 35 bg + 28 fg = 63 total

# 10 frames — 63 data elements each (35 bg + 28 fg), no traces parameter.
all_frames = []
for lami, lam in enumerate(lambdas_c):
    frame_data = []
    for bzi in range(n_bz):
        for k in range(K_c):
            frame_data.append(go.Scatter(y=list(bg_c_hats[(bzi, lami)][k] * mrange)))
    for bzi in range(n_bz):
        M_D, M_Y, bD_hat, bD_th = avp[(bzi, lami)]
        frame_data += [
            go.Scatter(x=list(M_D), y=list(M_Y)),
            go.Scatter(x=list(mrange), y=list(bD_true * mrange),
                       name=f"True β_D = {bD_true}",
                       line=dict(color="#739e7e", width=2.5)),
            go.Scatter(x=list(mrange), y=list(bD_hat * mrange),
                       name=f"OLS β̂_D = {bD_hat:.3f}",
                       line=dict(color="#8a6eb5", width=2.5, dash="dash")),
            go.Scatter(x=list(mrange), y=list(bD_th * mrange),
                       name=f"Theory = {bD_th:.3f}",
                       line=dict(color="#c4a8d4", width=1.5, dash="dot")),
        ]
    all_frames.append(go.Frame(name=f"lam{lami}", data=frame_data))

animate_args = dict(mode="immediate", frame=dict(duration=0, redraw=True),
                    transition=dict(duration=0))

lam_steps = [
    dict(method="animate", label=str(lam),
         args=[[f"lam{lami}"], animate_args])
    for lami, lam in enumerate(lambdas_c)
]

# β_Z restyle — 63 visibility values (35 bg then 28 fg)
bz_steps = []
for bzi_sel in range(n_bz):
    vis_list, sl_list = [], []
    for bzi in range(n_bz):
        for _ in range(K_c):
            vis_list.append(bzi == bzi_sel)
            sl_list.append(False)
    for bzi in range(n_bz):
        v = (bzi == bzi_sel)
        vis_list += [v,     v,    v,    v]
        sl_list  += [False, v,    v,    v]
    bz_steps.append(dict(
        method="restyle",
        label=f"{bz_vals[bzi_sel]:+.1f}",
        args=[{"visible": vis_list, "showlegend": sl_list}],
    ))

fig_c = go.Figure(
    data=init_traces,
    frames=all_frames,
    layout=go.Layout(
        xaxis=dict(title="D | Z* (residualized)", range=[-3, 3]),
        yaxis=dict(title="Y | Z* (residualized)", range=[-6, 6]),
        legend=dict(x=0.02, y=0.98, bgcolor="rgba(255,255,255,0.7)"),
        margin=dict(t=40, b=110),
        height=530,
        sliders=[
            dict(
                active=lam_full_idx,
                x=0.0, len=0.47, xanchor="left",
                currentvalue=dict(prefix="λ(Z*) = ", visible=True, xanchor="center"),
                pad=dict(b=10, t=40),
                steps=lam_steps,
            ),
            dict(
                active=default_bzi,
                x=0.53, len=0.47, xanchor="left",
                currentvalue=dict(prefix="β_Z = ", visible=True, xanchor="center"),
                pad=dict(b=10, t=40),
                steps=bz_steps,
            ),
        ],
    )
)
show(fig_c)
```

---

## 3. Instrumental Variables (IV) & 2SLS
Below, we will show how using instrumental
variables can recover unbiased estimates when there is (i) classical ME in the
treatment variable and (ii) classical ME in a confounding variable. 

### IV for Classical ME in Treatment Variables
To recover $\beta$ consistently despite noisy $D^*$, we need a second proxy
$T$ for the true $D$. The IV must satisfy three assumptions: 

1. **Exclusion**: $\text{Cov}(T, \varepsilon) = 0$ ($T$ affects $Y$ only
   through $D$)
2. **Exogeneity**: $U \perp T$ (measurement error in $D^*$ is independent of
the instrument $T$)
3. **Relevance**: $\text{Cov}(T, D^*) \neq 0$ ($T$ is correlated with
   the mismeasured treatment)

Remember that we want to determine $\beta$ in $Y =
\beta D + \varepsilon$, but we measure a noisy $D^* = D + U$. Then 

$$\text{Cov}(Y, T) = \text{Cov}(\beta D + \varepsilon, T) = \beta \text{Cov}(D,
T) $$

$$\text{Cov}(D^*, T) = \text{Cov}(D + U, T) =\text{Cov}(D,
T) $$

so $\hat{\beta} = \text{Cov}(Y, T) / \text{Cov}(D^*, T)$. 

In the first derivation, we leverage the exclusion assumption; in the second,
we leverage the exogeneity assumption. 

### Two-Stage Least Squares
In practice,
we can implement the estimator as a two-stage OLS (2SLS):

**Stage 1**: regress $D^*$ on $T$:

$$\hat D^* = \hat\pi_0 + \hat\pi_1 T$$

**Stage 2**: regress $Y$ on $\hat D^*$:

$$Y = \alpha + \beta\,\hat D^* + \eta$$

### Exercise: BMI and Blood Pressure

Below, you'll use 2SLS to see how
instrumental variables can recover $\beta_D$. We will compare
the results from a 2SLS regression to a regression with the ground truth
treatment. 

Suppose a researcher wants to quantify how much systolic blood pressure rises
per unit increase in BMI. The researcher suspects that reported BMI is subject
to measurement error due to scale calibration or rounding.

We treat the NHANES BMI column as ground truth and simulate two independent
noisy readings:

$$\text{BMI}^*_1 = \text{BMI} + U_1, \quad U_1 \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\,4^2)$$
$$\text{BMI}^*_2 = \text{BMI} + U_2, \quad U_2 \sim \mathcal{N}(1,\,10^2)$$

$\text{BMI}^*_1$ is the mismeasured proxy while $\text{BMI}^*_2$ is the
instrument. 

#### Step 0: Load data and simulate measurement error

```r
library(NHANES)
d <- na.omit(NHANES[, c("BPSysAve", "BMI")])

set.seed(9)
d$bmi_star <- d$BMI + rnorm(nrow(d), mean = 0, sd = 4)   # noisy regressor X*
d$iv       <- d$BMI + rnorm(nrow(d), mean = 1, sd = 10)  # instrument
```

#### Step 1: OLS benchmarks

```r
# Benchmark: OLS on true (unobserved) BMI
ols_true <- lm(BPSysAve ~ BMI, data = d)

# What we observe in practice: OLS on the noisy proxy
ols_fit  <- lm(BPSysAve ~ bmi_star, data = d)

summary(ols_true)
summary(ols_fit)
```

#### Step 2: 2SLS with `ivreg()`

```r
library(AER)
iv_fit <- ivreg(BPSysAve ~ bmi_star | iv, data = d)
summary(iv_fit, diagnostics = TRUE)
```

#### Step 3: Compare all three

```r
models <- list(
  "OLS (true BMI)"  = ols_true,
  "OLS (noisy BMI)" = ols_fit,
  "2SLS (ivreg)"    = iv_fit
)

results <- sapply(models, function(m) {
  b  <- round(coef(m)[2], 3)        # slope (2nd coef after intercept)
  se <- round(sqrt(diag(vcov(m)))[2], 3)
  c(estimate = b, se = se)
})

print(results)
```

::: {.callout-tip icon=false collapse=true}
## Expected results

| | OLS (true BMI) | OLS (noisy BMI) | 2SLS (`ivreg`) |
|---|---|---|---|
| BMI coefficient | 0.644 | ~0.499 | ~0.644 |
| SE | (0.028) | (0.024) | (~0.043) |

OLS on the noisy proxy attenuates the coefficient from 0.644 to ~0.499. The
reliability ratio is $\lambda = 7.4^2 / (7.4^2 + 4^2) \approx 0.77$. The 2SLS
recovers the true slope despite using noisy proxies. 
:::


### IV for Classical ME in Confounding Variables
We can also use instrumental variables to recover unbiased effects when there is
classical ME in a confounding variable.

Consider the structural model below, where $Z$ is the latent (never-observed)
confounder, $Z^*$ is a noisy proxy for $Z$, and
$\beta_0$ is the causal effect we want to estimate:

$$Y = \beta_0 D + \beta_2 Z + U_Y$$

$$D = \beta_1 Z + U_D$$

$$Z^* = \beta_3 Z + U_{Z^*}$$


We can express $\beta_0$ in terms of the variances and covariances of the
*observed* variables. 


After some algebra, we get

$$ \beta_0 = \frac{\text{Cov}(D,Y) -
\text{Cov}(D,Z^*)\,\text{Cov}(Z^*,Y)  /  \beta_3^2\,\text{Var}(Z)}{\text{Var}(D)
- \text{Cov}^2(D,Z^*) / \beta_3^2\,\text{Var}(Z)} \tag{1} $$

but we don't observe $\beta_3^2 \text{Var}(Z)$. With the second proxy $V$ we can
identify:

$$\beta_3^2 \text{Var}(Z) = \text{Cov}(D,Z^*)\,\text{Cov}(Z^*,V) / \text{Cov}(D,V)
\tag{2} $$

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Why does $\beta_3^2 \text{Var}(Z) = \text{Cov}(D,Z^*)\,\text{Cov}(Z^*,V) / \text{Cov}(D,V)$

Structural equation for our instrument: $V = \beta_4 Z + U_V$. We can derive the
following covariances: 

$$\text{Cov}(D, Z^*) = \beta_1 \beta_3 \text{Var}(Z)$$
$$\text{Cov}(D, V) = \beta_1 \beta_4 \text{Var}(Z)$$
$$\text{Cov}(Z^*, V) = \beta_3 \beta_4 \text{Var}(Z)$$

So we get

$$\frac{\text{Cov}(D, Z^*)\,\text{Cov}(Z^*, V)}{\text{Cov}(D, V)} = \frac{\beta_1 \beta_3 \text{Var}(Z) \cdot \beta_3 \beta_4 \text{Var}(Z)}{\beta_1 \beta_4 \text{Var}(Z)}= \beta_3^2 \text{Var}(Z) \tag{2}$$
:::

Substituting (2) into (1) yields an estimator that depends only on
observed covariances:

$$\beta_0 = \frac{\text{Cov}(D,Y)\,\text{Cov}(D,V) -
\text{Cov}(Y,Z^*)\,\text{Cov}(Z^*,V)}{\text{Cov}(D,V)\,\text{Var}(D) -
\text{Cov}(D,Z^*)\,\text{Cov}(Z^*,V)} \tag{3}$$


::: {.callout-note}
## Question
Without a second proxy $V$, equation (1) cannot be estimated from observed
data alone. What quantity do we need to know to estimate equation (1) without
a proxy?
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Answer

We need to know $\beta_3^2\,\text{Var}(Z)$. We don't observe this quantity
because $Z$ is unobserved. 
:::

## 4. Methods for Latent Variables
Sometimes the outcome refers to constructs that are not measured directly but
can be quantified using observed attributes or indicators. In this section, we will
show how researchers operationalize constructs of interest using
multiple observed indicators (e.g., questions in a test or a survey).

During implementation, researchers must make several decisions
based on substantive domain knowledge: (i) selecting the best indicators to
model the latent variable of interest, and (ii) summarizing the information from
those indicators. In applied research, researchers often rely on a composite score
that quantifies the degree to which a construct is manifested for each unit of
observation (e.g., sum scores, predicted scores, or imputed scores). Causal
inferences can be more or less biased depending not only on the chosen scoring
method but also on other research conditions, such as the number of indicators
and the magnitude of their measurement error.


The latent variable approach assumes that the
indicators share a common cause, allowing us to use the
common variance to model an underlying unobserved variable by specifying
a measurement model. This measurement model represents how observed
variables are linked to a latent variable. Additionally, structural
models can represent how observed variables (outside the
measurement model) and latent variables connect to one another. This
framework empirically contrasts substantive knowledge with data using
structural equation modeling (SEM; Bollen, 1989), in which multiple
equations are estimated simultaneously.

Below, we present a simplified example of incorporating a
measurement model specification within the SEM approach.

### Case 1: Observed Treatment to Latent Continuous Outcome

In this baseline scenario, the treatment condition $X \in \{0, 1\}$ is
perfectly measured and assigned, but the outcome is a latent construct
$\eta_Y$ measured by a set of continuous indicators.

By modeling the outcome as latent, the causal estimand (ATE) is evaluated on the
true latent scale. This avoids the attenuation bias inherent in traditional
two-step scoring strategies (such as using sum scores or extracted factor scores
as an observed outcome in a standard regression).

#### 1. Measurement Model Specification

For an individual $i$ and item $j$, the measurement model is:

$$y_{ij} = \nu_j + \lambda_j \eta_{Yi} + \epsilon_{ij}$$

Where the item residual is
$\epsilon_{ij} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \theta_j)$. 

To identify the model and
set the metric of $\eta_Y$, we can either (i) standardize the latent
variable prior to estimating the structural paths
($\eta_{Yi} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \psi)$) or (ii) fix a reference factor
loading (e.g., $\lambda_1 = 1$).

#### 2. Structural Model Specification

The structural parameterization directly regresses the latent variable
on the observed binary treatment:

$$\eta_{Yi} = \alpha_Y + \tau X_i + \zeta_i$$

Where $\zeta_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \psi_{residual})$, and $\tau$
represents the ATE on the latent scale.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Case 2: Latent Treatment Assignment

This second scenario maps onto selection mechanisms where an underlying latent
propensity, $\eta_X$, determines the true treatment assignment. This continuous
propensity is discretized into an observed binary treatment $X$, which
subsequently causes the observed continuous outcome $Y$.

#### 1. Latent Propensity Measurement Model

The continuous latent condition is measured by a set of indicators $k$:

$$\boldsymbol{x}_{ik} = \boldsymbol{\nu}_{Xk} + \boldsymbol{\lambda}_{Xk} \eta_{Xi} + \boldsymbol{\delta}_{ik}$$

Where $\eta_{Xi} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)$ represents the underlying
continuous condition. For example, when strict experimental conditions
are difficult to meet, we might use a set of questions to determine
whether participants are adhering to a specific treatment (e.g., dietary
restrictions). We need sufficient information to classify them into the
categorical conditions of interest. To do so, a threshold must be
established.

#### 2. Discretization and Treatment Assignment Mechanism

The latent condition dictates treatment assignment via an indicator
function using a fixed threshold $c$:

$$X_i = I(\eta_{Xi} > c)$$

*(Note:* $X_i = 1$ if the condition is met, and $X_i = 0$ otherwise).

#### 3. Observed Outcome Model

The final structural model evaluates the effect of the assigned
treatment on the observed continuous outcome:

$$Y_i = \alpha_Y + \tau X_i + \epsilon_i$$

Where $\epsilon_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2_Y)$.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 5. Measurement Invariance

Concerns may arise regarding whether scores (i.e., measures) derived from the
latent variable framework are comparable across different groups. For example,
we must assess whether the observed responses are caused by the same latent
variable across groups, and whether that latent variable causes variation of the
same magnitude for all indicators.

When utilizing a measurement model, we must ensure it meets specific
invariance requirements. Essentially, this guarantees that the
measurement properties do not vary because of group-related
characteristics. Measurement invariance operates on a progressive
spectrum, meaning a measure can demonstrate anything from weak to strict
invariance depending on the parameters being constrained. Crucially,
these levels are nested; if a lower level of invariance is not met, a
higher level cannot be achieved. Below is a brief description of these
levels, building from the lowest requirement:

### Configural Invariance

This stage is designed to test if the model has the same basic form
across groups, implying that the pattern of relationships between the
indicators and the latent variable is equivalent. Achieving this level
means the conceptual structure is reflected similarly across groups.

### Metric Invariance

Once there is evidence that the model form is the same, the next step is
to check for equivalence in the magnitude to which the indicators relate
to the latent variable. This is tested by constraining the factor
loadings to be equal across groups.

### Scalar Invariance

This level of invariance implies that mean differences in the latent
construct capture all mean differences in the shared variance of the
indicators. For example, if we have an item asking about the frequency
of a specific behavior, a scalar-invariant measure ensures that
individuals from different groups with the exact same level of the
latent trait will endorse the item with the same average frequency. A
non-invariant measure would mean that for some groups, the average
observed frequency is higher or lower without a corresponding true
difference in the underlying latent variable.


### Residual Invariance

The final step to establish measurement invariance is to test if the
item residuals are equivalent. In other words, we constrain the specific
variance and measurement error to be equal across groups. Although this
level demonstrates strict invariance, it is not strictly required to
compare latent means, since the residuals are distinct from the latent
factor.

*(Note: An alternative approach to establishing a measurement model
falls under Item Response Theory (IRT), in which a probability function
formalizes the relationship between a categorical observed response and
a continuous underlying latent variable. The invariance property can
also be tested within this framework, where a lack of invariance is more
commonly referred to as Differential Item Functioning, or DIF.)*

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## 6. Takeaways

- Before reasoning about confounding and generalizability, researchers often
assume they measured experimental variables accurately.

- This assumption is not always reasonable, especially when measuring abstract
constructs or using instruments subject to human error.

- Ignoring measurement error can bias outcomes even when there is no confounding
present in the DAG

- Well designed experiments (e.g., carefully selected instrumental variables)
can help detect unbiased effects when researchers suspect measurement error.


- We can use proxy variables as latent variables if we can model a valid relationship
between the proxy and latent variable


---

## Sources

An, L. (2025). *Measurement in K-12 policy analysis* (Doctoral dissertation, Harvard University). <https://dash.harvard.edu/server/api/core/bitstreams/7035e654-685f-4d1a-837e-e66ab492c665/content>

Bulbulia, J. A. (2024). Methods in causal inference. Part 3: Measurement error and external validity threats. *Evolutionary Human Sciences*, *6*, e42. <https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4723683/pdf/dyu272.pdf>

de Romémont, J. (2024). *Measurement in data science* [Lecture notes]. <https://uclspp.github.io/POLS0013/2-measurement-theory-and-error.html>

Edwards, J. K., Cole, S. R., & Westreich, D. (2015). All your data are always missing: incorporating bias due to measurement error into the potential outcomes framework. *International Journal of Epidemiology*, *44*(4), 1452–1459. <https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11588564/>

Jovanović, M. (n.d.). *Measurement error*. bmbstats Book. <https://mladenjovanovic.github.io/bmbstats-book/measurement-error.html>

Lauderdale, B. E. (in press). *Pragmatic social measurement*. <https://uclspp.github.io/POLS0013/00_textbook/pragmatic-social-measurement.pdf>

Lemieux, T. (2019). *ECON 594: Lecture 8* [Lecture notes]. University of British Columbia. <https://econ2017.sites.olt.ubc.ca/files/2019/05/pdf_594lecture8_thomas-lemieux.pdf>

Li, F. (2024). *Instrumental variables* [Lecture slides]. Duke University. <https://www2.stat.duke.edu/~fl35/teaching/640/Chapter6.1_InstrumentalVariables.pdf>

Machielsen, B. (n.d.). *Measurement error*. <https://basmachielsen.nl/posts/measurement_error>

Hanck, C., Arnold, M., Gerber, A., & Schmelzer, M. (n.d.). *Instrumental variables regression*. In *Introduction to econometrics with R* (Ch. 12). <https://www.econometrics-with-r.org/12-ivr.html>

Pearl, J. (2012). On measurement bias in causal inference. *arXiv:1203.3504*. <https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.1203.3504>

Pischke, S. (2007). *Lecture notes on measurement error* [Lecture notes]. London School of Economics. <https://econ.lse.ac.uk/staff/spischke/ec524/Merr_new.pdf>

Rubin, E. (n.d.). *ARE 212: Section 11 — Measurement error* [Course notes]. University of California, Berkeley. <https://edrub.in/ARE212/section11.html#measurement_error>

The Econometrics Blog. (n.d.). *Beyond classical measurement error*. <https://www.econometrics.blog/post/beyond-classical-measurement-error/>
